tldr: Cloudflare is a global CDN, DNS, security, and edge-compute platform, while Yandex Cloud is a Russian hyperscaler with CDN, compute, storage, and ML services. Teams typically migrate to consolidate inside Yandex Cloud for data residency, sanctions, and procurement reasons rather than for feature parity.
why_switch: Russian data residency for regulatory compliance
Better latency for users in Russia and CIS
Co-locate CDN with Yandex compute and storage
export_method: Use Terraform's Cloudflare provider or the REST API to export DNS records, cache and ruleset configuration, WAF rules, and Worker routes as code. Worker source lives in your wrangler project; capture each Worker, its routes, and its bindings (KV, D1, R2, Queues). For R2 origins, mirror objects to Yandex Object Storage using S3-compatible tooling such as rclone or aws s3 sync against the R2 endpoint. Pull a sample of Cloudflare access logs from Logpush so you can compare cache hit ratios after cutover. TLS certificates from Cloudflare cannot be exported; plan to re-issue on Yandex Cloud. Confirm exact Cloudflare API endpoints and Terraform resources against current Cloudflare documentation.
import_method: In Yandex Cloud, create CDN resources per hostname with origins pointing at your existing infrastructure or at Yandex Object Storage. Translate Cloudflare cache rules into Yandex CDN configuration, simplifying where features have no parity. Move R2 contents to Yandex Object Storage with S3-compatible tooling. Re-implement Workers either at the origin or as Yandex Cloud Functions / API Gateway, accounting for runtime differences. Issue or upload TLS certificates via Yandex Cloud's certificate workflow. Cut DNS over one hostname at a time with lowered TTLs and side-by-side log comparison. Verify exact Yandex Cloud feature names and Function bindings against current Yandex Cloud documentation.

concept_mapping.target: DNS provider (external or Yandex Cloud DNS)
Yandex Cloud Functions or origin logic

effort: 3-8 weeks depending on Workers footprint, R2 data volume, and number of zones
gotchas: Workers have no drop-in equivalent; refactor to origin logic or Yandex Cloud Functions
Yandex Cloud's PoP footprint is RU/CIS-focused; non-CIS users may see worse latency
Sanctions and payment restrictions can block non-Russian customers from contracting with Yandex Cloud
Bundled Cloudflare features (Access, Bot Management, full DNS proxying) require separate replacements
TLS certs from Cloudflare cannot be exported; certificates must be re-issued
S3-compatible parity between R2 and Yandex Object Storage is not exact; validate large-object and multipart workflows

email.subject: Winding down our Cloudflare usage over the next 14 days
email.body:
Hi Cloudflare team,

We are migrating our CDN, DNS, and edge workloads off Cloudflare onto Yandex Cloud over the next 14 days, mostly for data residency and commercial reasons in our Russia/CIS footprint.

In this window we plan to mirror R2 buckets to Yandex Object Storage, re-create cache rules and TLS on Yandex CDN, refactor Worker logic to our origin or Yandex Cloud Functions, and cut DNS over hostname by hostname with lowered TTLs and parallel logs. Cloudflare will remain live as a fallback during cutover.

Could you confirm how long historical analytics, logs, and billing data remain accessible after we stop traffic, plus the cleanest order to disable Workers, R2, and zones to avoid surprise charges or data loss?

Thanks for the support.

Your customer
